perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The modification shortens the confidentiality period to one year, which may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under North Carolina law, potentially conflicting with reasonable measures to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: North Carolina's Trade Secrets Protection Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and a one-year confidentiality period may not be considered reasonable for certain trade secrets, thereby undermining the protection intended by the law.
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both assert that a one-year period might be insufficient to protect trade secrets under North Carolina law, potentially conflicting with the reasonable measures required to maintain secrecy. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the time-sensitive obligation (confidentiality period) in the contract may not align with legal requirements, potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable. While there are no scraped snippets to directly confirm the specific required timeframe, the provided explanations strongly indicate that this change introduces a contradiction because the shortened period may undermine the protection intended by the law.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, Cyberlux and ICT intend to enter into discussions concerning a business relationship whereby ICT will provide Cyberlux with certain services; NOW, THEREFORE, the parties hereto agree as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, Cyberlux and ICT intend to enter into discussions concerning a business relationship whereby ICT will provide Cyberlux with certain services, including labor; NOW, THEREFORE, the parties hereto agree as follows: ICT is not responsible for worker's compensation.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text attempts to waive responsibility for worker's compensation, conflicting with North Carolina law that mandates worker's compensation coverage for employees.
perturbation.law_explanation: North Carolina law requires employers to provide worker's compensation insurance for their employees. The modified contract language stating ICT is not responsible for worker's compensation directly violates this mandatory obligation, rendering that clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: North Carolina General Statutes § 97-9 (2023) - Employer to secure payment of compensation. :: 2023 North Carolina General Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia Go to Previous Versions of this Section 2023 N.C. Gen. Stat. (here) 2022 N.C. Gen. Stat. 2021 N.C. Gen. Stat. View All Versions 2023 North Carolina General Statutes Chapter 97 - Workers' Compensation Act Article 1 - Workers' Compensation Act. § 97-9 - Employer to secure payment of compensation. Universal Citation: NC Gen Stat § 97-9 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 97-9. Employer to secure payment of compensation. Every employer subject to the compensation provisions of this Article shall secure the payment of compensation to his employees in the manner hereinafter provided; and while such security remains in force, he or those conducting his business shall only be liable to any employee for personal injury or death by accident to the extent and in the manner herein specified. (1929, c. 120, s. 10; 1973, c. 1291, s. 5.) Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. North Carolina may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text explicitly states that ICT is 'not responsible for worker's compensation,' which directly contradicts North Carolina General Statutes § 97-9. This statute mandates that 'Every employer subject to the compensation provisions of this Article shall secure the payment of compensation to his employees.' The changed text attempts to waive a legally required obligation, establishing a clear legal contradiction because it attempts to circumvent a mandatory obligation under North Carolina law.

perturbation.original_text: Any documents or materials that are furnished by or on behalf of the disclosing party, and all other Proprietary Information in whatever form, including documents, reports, memoranda, notes, files or analyses prepared by or on behalf of the receiving party, including all copies of such materials, shall be promptly returned by the receiving party to the disclosing party upon written request by the disclosing party for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: Any documents or materials that are furnished by or on behalf of the disclosing party, and all other Proprietary Information in whatever form, including documents, reports, memoranda, notes, files or analyses prepared by or on behalf of the receiving party, including all copies of such materials, shall be destroyed by the receiving party upon written request by the disclosing party for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: The change mandates destruction of documents upon request, potentially conflicting with document retention requirements under North Carolina law, especially if related to litigation or investigations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Rule of Civil Procedure 45
perturbation.law_explanation: N.C. R. Civ. P. 45(c)(2)(B) allows a person subpoenaed to produce documents to object to the request, including a request for destruction of materials. Mandating destruction upon request without regard to legal holds or objections contradicts these procedural rights and potential discovery obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. perturbation.justification: The modified text mandates the destruction of documents upon request, replacing the original obligation to return them. The Law Explanation indicates this contradicts N.C. R. Civ. P. 45(c)(2)(B), which allows a person subpoenaed to produce documents to object to the request, including a request for destruction of materials. The provided snippet from Rule 45 discusses subpoenas and the production of documents, implying a legal process for handling documents that could be circumvented by mandatory destruction. By mandating destruction, the changed text potentially undermines legal procedures and rights related to document production, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: b. The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, as well as the legal relations of the parties arising hereunder, will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Carolina.
perturbation.changed_text: b. The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, as well as the legal relations of the parties arising hereunder, will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law from North Carolina to Delaware, and excluding conflict of laws principles, could potentially violate North Carolina's public policy if the agreement directly and substantially affects real property or other significant interests located in North Carolina.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina choice of law rules and public policy
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties generally can choose governing law, North Carolina courts may refuse to enforce a choice of law provision if it violates a fundamental public policy of North Carolina. If this agreement involves substantial contacts with North Carolina, such as real property or employment relationships, applying Delaware law could undermine North Carolina's regulatory interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 1-75.3 § 1-75.3. Jurisdictional requirements for judgments against persons, status and things. (a) Jurisdiction of Subject Matter Not Affected by This Article. - Nothing in this Article shall be construed to confer, enlarge or diminish the subject matter jurisdiction of any court. (b) Personal Jurisdiction. - A court of this State having jurisdiction of the subject matter may render a judgment against a party personally only if there exists one or more of the jurisdictional grounds set forth in G.S. 1-75.4 or G.S. 1-75.7 and in addition either: (1) Personal service or substituted personal service of summons, or service of publication of a notice of service of process is made upon the defendant pursuant to Rule 4(j) or Rule 4(j1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure; or (2) Service of a summons is dispensed with under the conditions in G.S. 1-75.7. (c) Jurisdiction in Rem or Quasi in Rem. - A court of this State having jurisdiction of the subject matter may render a judgment in rem or quasi in rem upon a status or upon a property or other things pursuant to G.S. 1-75.8 and the judgment in such action may affect the interests in the status, property or thing of all persons served pursuant to Rule 4(k) of the Rules of Civil Procedure. (1967, c. 954, s. 2; 1983, c. 231.)
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that North Carolina law governs the agreement. The changed text switches this to Delaware law and excludes conflict of laws principles. The Perturbation and Law Explanations both highlight that this change could violate North Carolina's public policy, especially if the agreement has substantial contacts with North Carolina, such as real property. G.S. 1-75.3(a) states that the article does not affect subject matter jurisdiction, suggesting North Carolina retains some jurisdictional interest. While the snippet does not directly forbid choice-of-law changes, the explanations taken together support the determination that changing the governing law could undermine North Carolina's regulatory interests, thus contradicting NC public policy.
